name: Suraqa ibn Malik
title: Suraqa ibn Malik (RA): The Bounty Hunter
meta_description: The story of Suraqa ibn Malik (RA), the desert tracker who chased the Prophet Muhammad for a bounty and was promised the bracelets of the most powerful king on earth. How a hunter became a believer.
keywords: Suraqa ibn Malik, story of Suraqa, the bounty hunter, Hijra, bracelets of Kisra, sahaba stories, the firsts
lede: A man was riding hard across the open desert, tracking the most wanted person in Arabia. There was a fortune waiting for whoever brought him in, and no one read the sand better than he did. Then, three times, the ground itself seemed to refuse to let him close the distance.

This is the story of Suraqa ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him): the tracker who set out to capture the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ for a bounty, and rode home instead with a written pledge of safety and a promise he could not yet believe.

paras: Suraqa ibn Malik was a tracker by trade. He knew the desert the way other men knew the streets of a city. He could read footprints in the sand, human and animal alike, and tell you how many had passed through a place and when, just by the marks they left behind.

He was the kind of man who scouted camps before a battle and counted troops the same way. There are people whose whole interest is the craft of the battlefield, before and after Islam, and nothing else quite holds them. Suraqa was one of these. War and the reading of the land were his world.

He lived on the outskirts of Makkah, among people who were not city folk. He was a skilled horseman, at home in the empty places where others lost their way. So when the message of Islam began to spread through Makkah, Suraqa paid it no attention at all. It was simply not his concern.

sections.h2: A bounty of a hundred camels
paras: When the Prophet ﷺ slipped out of Makkah and made his Hijra, his escape became known, and Abu Jahl announced a bounty on his head. The reward was a hundred camels, contributed by the tribes: the finest animals they could offer.

It is hard to overstate what that meant. A hundred of the finest camels would make one man wealthy for the rest of his life: the equivalent, in that world, of a fortune in luxury. Every tribe would put forward its best, and one man would carry all of them home.

The reports say as many as four hundred people went out searching. Many of them did not care about his message at all. They had no quarrel with his theology; they simply wanted the prize, and the bounty was the same whether they brought him back alive or dead.

Suraqa, who had ignored the whole affair of Islam, heard the figure and thought only one thing: that is me. No one could track a man through the desert better than he could, and he knew this land. He said he would find the fugitive and bring him back within three nights.

sections.h2: The horse that would not carry him
paras: He traced the trail and at last saw the Prophet ﷺ in the distance. As the Prophet ﷺ looked back toward him, Suraqa felt his horse suddenly sink into the ground and throw him off. That had never happened to him before.

Something was strange here, he thought. Something was clearly going on with this man. But a hundred camels was a hundred camels. He climbed back on and rode again, and this time the horse sank deeper and threw him harder than before.

Now he was confused. He had no religion to guide him, so he leaned on luck the way the Arabs did. He drew out his arrows and cast lots, the desert way of flipping a coin or rolling dice for an answer. The arrows told him to turn back, to leave this man alone.

He went anyway. "I will not let a hundred camels go," he told himself. He drew so close that he could hear the Prophet ﷺ, and the Prophet ﷺ could hear him. There was no distance left; he only had to rope him in. The Prophet ﷺ rode on, unbothered by the hunter behind him. Then, a final time, the horse threw Suraqa to the ground.

Now Suraqa called out. He asked the Prophet ﷺ to pray to his Lord to release him, and gave his word that he would do no harm. He only wanted to come and speak. The Prophet ﷺ made the supplication, and Suraqa was able to ride up to him.

When he reached him, the hunter did not reach for a rope. He said that whatever this affair was, it was clearly going to spread across the world. So he asked for something strange for a man in his position to ask: a written guarantee of safety, for the day this message reached his own land.

The Prophet ﷺ, in the most vulnerable position a man could be in, hunted from every direction, gave it to him. He could have taken a spear and struck the hunter down where he stood. But he had a larger way of thinking and a wider heart, and so he wrote out the pledge of protection and handed it over.

Something had shifted in Suraqa. He no longer cared about the camels at all. The man who had set out for a fortune now wanted only one thing: do not harm me when your day comes, when Allah gives you the victory I can already sense is coming. Just let me be safe.

sections.h2: A promise in the open sand
paras: Suraqa offered the Prophet ﷺ his own provisions. He told him to take his arrow as a sign to the servants who guarded his camels, and to take whatever animals and help he wished. The Prophet ﷺ answered simply that he had no need of them, and told him to go.
As the hunter turned to leave him alone in the desert, the Prophet ﷺ called after him with a question. How would it be, Suraqa, on the day you wear the bracelets of Kisra?

Kisra was the ruler of Persia, the most powerful man in the world. His crown, his robe, and above all his golden bracelets were among the most precious possessions on earth. Suraqa looked back, half wondering if there were some other Kisra he had never heard of. The Prophet ﷺ smiled at him, and walked away.

It is worth pausing on the strangeness of that scene. A fugitive, alone in a remote stretch of desert, hunted by hundreds of men, with no army and no shelter, had just turned aside the one weapon that mattered: his own greed for a hundred camels.

And then, instead of pleading for his life, this fugitive offered the hunter something larger than the bounty. Not a hundred camels, but the most precious belonging of the most powerful king on earth. He said it plainly, and then he simply smiled and went on his way, as though it were already settled.

Suraqa rode off the way he had come. He had set out willing to kill the Prophet ﷺ for money. He was leaving with a written promise of safety in his hand and a far stranger promise turning over in his mind. He did not understand the religion, and he did not pretend to. He only understood that whatever this affair was, it was remarkable, and that it was going to spread across the world. That was enough to make him want to be on the right side of it when it came.

sections.h2: The day the promise came true
paras: Suraqa kept the paper. He went back to Makkah and held on to it through everything that followed. Years later, when Makkah was conquered and the city had changed hands, he came out to the Prophet ﷺ with that same document in his hand and said, remember this. He had waited all that time simply to bring it back. He bore witness that there is one God and that the Prophet ﷺ is His messenger, and so the hunter became a companion.

Time passed. The Prophet ﷺ died, and his successor after him. Then the long contest between the Muslims and the Persians came to its end. For centuries Persia had been at war with the Romans, and it looked down on the Muslims as bedouins of the desert, picking at them and attacking them from every side.

In a single year, that empire fell. The Muslims entered its great white palace at Madain, the largest palace in the world at the time, and its treasures were gathered and carried back. The reign of Kisra was over.

When the spoils were laid before the leader of the believers, among them were the bracelets of Kisra. He called for Suraqa, placed those golden bracelets on the wrists of the old tracker, and reminded the people that the one who had foretold this had spoken the truth. The most improbable transfer on earth had come true before their eyes. The hunter who once chased the Prophet ﷺ for a hundred camels had been found, instead, by a far greater blessing.

lessons_intro: Suraqa rode out for money and rode home with something he could not name yet. His story is short, but it carries a few lessons worth keeping. It is about what happens when a hard heart meets patience instead of force.

lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ could have struck the hunter down. He chose to write him a pledge of safety instead, and that mercy is what stayed with Suraqa for a decade.

lessons.bold: A closed heart can still be reached.
lessons.text: Suraqa had ignored the whole message of Islam. He was not looking for guidance. Yet one honest encounter began to turn him.

lessons.bold: Hold on to a promise quietly.
lessons.text: He kept the paper for years and waited. When the time came, he did not boast; he simply said, remember this, and gave his witness.

lessons.bold: Allah's promises do not depend on the odds.
lessons.text: A fugitive in the sand spoke of a distant king's treasure, and years later it rested on an old man's wrists. What seems impossible is not a measure of what is true.

faq.a: Suraqa ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a skilled desert tracker from the area around Makkah who set out to capture the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ during the Hijra for the bounty placed on him. He later accepted Islam and became a companion.

faq.q: Why was Suraqa chasing the Prophet Muhammad?
faq.a: After the Hijra, Abu Jahl announced a bounty of a hundred camels for capturing the Prophet ﷺ, dead or alive. Suraqa, known as the best tracker in the desert, went out to claim it.

faq.q: What was the prophecy about the bracelets of Kisra?
faq.a: As Suraqa turned back, the Prophet ﷺ asked him how it would be on the day he wore the bracelets of Kisra, the ruler of Persia. Years after the Prophet's death, when Persia fell, those golden bracelets were placed on Suraqa's own hands.

faq.q: What can we learn from Suraqa's story?
faq.a: That mercy can reach a hard heart where force cannot, that no one is beyond guidance, and that the promises of Allah hold true no matter how unlikely they seem.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Suraqa came as a hunter and left as a guest. The man he meant to capture met his greed with calm, his threat with mercy, and his closed heart with a promise instead of a blade. None of it was forced, and all of it landed. A heart that was not even listening began, in one encounter, to turn.

Most of us meet people the way Suraqa met the Prophet ﷺ: at the worst moment, with the wrong intentions somewhere in the room. His story asks us to be the one who answers hardness with patience, who does not strike when we easily could, and who keeps faith that what is true will outlast the odds against it. We rarely get to see our own bracelets of Kisra arrive. We are simply asked to act as though the promise is real. Carry that into a single difficult encounter today, and this story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Suraqa ibn Malik (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
A bounty was placed on the most wanted man in Arabia.

A hundred camels. Enough to make one man rich for life.

The best tracker in the desert said: that is me.

Suraqa ibn Malik could read footprints in the sand.

He could count troops by the marks they left.

He had ignored the message of Islam completely. This was just a hunt.

He found the Prophet ﷺ in the distance.

Then his horse sank into the ground and threw him.

He tried again. It sank deeper. A third time. Deeper still.

So the hunter called out, not for a capture, but for help.

"Pray to your Lord to free me, and I give you my word: I will do no harm."

He did not ask for the camels anymore.

He asked for a written pledge of safety, for the day this message reached his land.

The fugitive, hunted from every side, wrote it out and handed it to him.

As Suraqa turned to leave, the Prophet ﷺ called after him:

"How will it be, Suraqa, on the day you wear the bracelets of Kisra?"

The treasure of the most powerful king on earth.

He kept that paper for years.

A decade later he came forward, held it up, and said: remember this.

And he bore witness. The hunter had become a believer.

**Allah's promises do not depend on the odds.**

When Persia fell, the golden bracelets of Kisra were placed on the wrists of an old desert tracker.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Suraqa ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was not looking for guidance. He was a desert tracker, and when a bounty of a hundred camels was placed on the Prophet ﷺ after the Hijra, he set out to claim it.

Three times his horse threw him as he closed in. So he called out, not to capture, but to ask for safety. The Prophet ﷺ, hunted from every direction, could have struck him down. Instead he wrote him a pledge of protection, and told him that one day he would wear the bracelets of the most powerful king on earth.

Suraqa kept that promise folded away for years. When Persia fell, those golden bracelets were placed on his own hands.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The bounty hunter who chased the Prophet
email.alt_subject: "How will it be when you wear the bracelets of Kisra?"
email.preview: He rode out to capture the Prophet for a fortune. He rode home with a promise he could not yet believe.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Suraqa ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a desert tracker. He could read footprints in the sand and count troops by the marks they left. He had paid no attention at all to the message of Islam. It simply was not his concern.

Then the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ made his Hijra, and a bounty was placed on his head: a hundred camels, enough to make one man wealthy for life. Suraqa heard the figure and thought only one thing. That is me. No one could track a man through the desert better than he could.

He found the Prophet ﷺ in the distance. But as he closed in, his horse sank into the ground and threw him. He tried again, and it threw him harder. A third time, the same. So the hunter called out, not to capture, but to ask for help. Pray to your Lord to release me, he said, and I give you my word that I will do no harm.

When he reached the Prophet ﷺ, he did not reach for a rope. He asked instead for a written pledge of safety, for the day this message would reach his own land. The Prophet ﷺ, hunted from every direction and entirely within reach, could have struck him down. Instead he wrote out the pledge and handed it over.

And as Suraqa turned to leave, the Prophet ﷺ called after him with a question. How will it be, Suraqa, on the day you wear the bracelets of Kisra: the treasure of the most powerful king on earth? The hunter looked back, half wondering if there were some other king he had never heard of.

He kept that paper for years. When Makkah was conquered, he came forward, held it up, and said: remember this. And he bore witness that there is one God and that this was His messenger. The hunter had become a believer.

Years later, when Persia fell, the golden bracelets of Kisra were carried back with the spoils, and placed on the wrists of the old desert tracker. The most improbable promise had come true.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: mercy can reach a heart that force never could. The Prophet ﷺ answered a threat with patience, and it stayed with Suraqa for a decade.

May Allah be pleased with him, and soften our hearts the way his was softened.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: A man was riding hard across the open desert, tracking the most wanted person in all of Arabia. There was a fortune waiting for whoever brought him in.

Suraqa ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a tracker by trade. He could read footprints in the sand and count troops by the marks they left. He had never paid the message of Islam any attention. But when a bounty of a hundred camels was placed on the Prophet ﷺ after the Hijra, he heard the figure and thought only one thing: that is me.

He found the Prophet ﷺ in the distance. But as he rode closer, his horse sank into the ground and threw him off. He tried again, and it threw him harder. A third time, the same. Something strange was happening, and a hundred camels no longer felt like enough reason to keep going.

So the hunter called out, not to capture, but to ask for help. "Pray to your Lord to free me," he said, "and I give you my word that I will do no harm." The Prophet ﷺ supplicated, and Suraqa was able to ride up to him at last.

When he reached him, he did not reach for a rope. He asked instead for a written promise of safety, for the day this message would come to his land. The Prophet ﷺ, hunted from every side and entirely within reach, could have struck him down. Instead he wrote out the pledge and handed it over, and asked for nothing in return.

As Suraqa turned to leave him alone in the sand, the Prophet ﷺ called after him. "How will it be, Suraqa, on the day you wear the bracelets of Kisra?" The treasure of the most powerful king on earth, promised to a hunter by a fugitive in the desert. The Prophet ﷺ smiled, and walked away.
Suraqa kept that paper for years. When the time came, he came forward, held it up, and said: remember this. And he bore witness, and the hunter became a believer.
Long after, when Persia fell, the golden bracelets of Kisra were carried back and placed on the wrists of the old tracker, exactly as he had been told.
storybook.pullquote: How will it be, Suraqa, on the day you wear the bracelets of Kisra?
storybook.reflection: The Prophet ﷺ met a hunter who came for his life, and answered him with mercy instead of a blade. That mercy, not force, is what turned a closed heart. When someone meets us at our worst, with the wrong intentions, we can still choose patience over the strike we are entitled to, and trust that what is true will outlast the odds.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
